coredata.dc:description: The misuse of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics leads to pollution of the water environment, necessitating the development of cost-effective and efficient degradation methods. This article presents a one-step hydrothermal method (200℃, 8 h) for the preparation of Fe-N-co-doped biochar/carbon quantum dots (Fe/N/BC/CQDs) as a photocatalytic material. The Fe/N/BC/CQDs was employed for the activation of H2O and O2, facilitating the degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics, with superoxide radicals, photo-generated holes, hydroxyl radicals, and singlet oxygen dominated the photodegradation process. Under 1 h dark and 8 h natural light conditions, the degradation efficiency of norfloxacin, sulfathiazole, sulfamethoxazole, sulfamonomethoxine, sulfadoxin, sulfamethazine (10 mg/L) by Fe/N/BC/CQDs ranged from 74.21 % to 89.23 %. The potential degradation pathways of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics were proposed based on ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography tandem high-resolution mass spectrometry analysis of the intermediates. The Fe-N-doped biochar’s hierarchical pore structure enhances synergistic adsorption-catalysis effects, while the incorporated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) broaden solar spectrum utilization, effectively overcoming the high-intensity light dependency common to conventional photocatalysts. Efficient charge separation was achieved through the formation of a p-n heterojunction between Fe-N-co-doped biochar and CQDs. Additionally, the applicability of Fe/N/BC/CQDs in real water systems was confirmed, providing valuable insights for the construction of p-n heterojunction structures to degrade organic pollutants in water.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics by Fe-N-co-doped biochar/carbon quantum dots p-n heterojunction materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The misuse of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics leads to pollution of the water environment, necessitating the development of cost-effective and efficient degradation methods. This article presents a one-step hydrothermal method (200℃, 8 h) for the preparation of Fe-N-co-doped biochar/carbon quantum dots (Fe/N/BC/CQDs) as a photocatalytic material. The Fe/N/BC/CQDs was employed for the activation of H2O and O2, facilitating the degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics, with superoxide radicals, photo-generated holes, hydroxyl radicals, and singlet oxygen dominated the photodegradation process. Under 1 h dark and 8 h natural light conditions, the degradation efficiency of norfloxacin, sulfathiazole, sulfamethoxazole, sulfamonomethoxine, sulfadoxin, sulfamethazine (10 mg/L) by Fe/N/BC/CQDs ranged from 74.21 % to 89.23 %. The potential degradation pathways of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics were proposed based on ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography tandem high-resolution mass spectrometry analysis of the intermediates. The Fe-N-doped biochar’s hierarchical pore structure enhances synergistic adsorption-catalysis effects, while the incorporated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) broaden solar spectrum utilization, effectively overcoming the high-intensity light dependency common to conventional photocatalysts. Efficient charge separation was achieved through the formation of a p-n heterojunction between Fe-N-co-doped biochar and CQDs. Additionally, the applicability of Fe/N/BC/CQDs in real water systems was confirmed, providing valuable insights for the construction of p-n heterojunction structures to degrade organic pollutants in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been extensively used in medical treatments for humans and animals in recent years, and their global consumption was estimated at 225,000 tons in 2020 [1]. Sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics, two widely used antibiotics in diseases caused by bacterial infections [2], can not be fully metabolized by human body, leading to their discharge into the water environment [3]. For example, sulfamethoxazole (SMZ) and norfloxacin (NOR) were found in the effluents of 14 wastewater treatment plants distributed across China, with concentrations of 102.3 − 3930.8 ng/L and 14.8 − 2766.2 ng/L, respectively [4]. Sulfamethazine (SM2) was also detected in surface water, ranging from 0.065 µg/L to 6.19 µg/L [5]. The sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics pollution might have negative impacts on aquatic ecosystems [6]. Chronic exposure to antibiotics at both low environmental concentrations (260 ng/L for SMZ) and legal aquaculture doses (100 mg/kg body weight/day for SMZ) severely impaired physiological functions, nutritional metabolism, and immune system health in Nile tilapia (Oreochromis niloticus) fingerlings [7]. Wei et al. (2024) also found that sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics in the Laizhou Bay posed a moderate to high risk to algae [8]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop methods for efficiently removing sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics from aquatic environments.

Photocatalysis was considered to be the most appropriate method to remove stable and non-biodegradable organic pollutants compared to biological, electrochemical and membrane processes [9,10]. Waste biomass materials-based biochar (BC) exhibited typical semiconductor properties, owing to its condensed aromatic skeleton, sp2/sp3 hybrid structure and functional groups [11]. These characteristics enabled BC to function as multifunctional catalysts under light conditions, making it a subject of widespread attention on photocatalytic degradation of pollutants in recent years [12]. A Fe/Ti BC prepared from wood was applied to photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin and NOR in the water, achieving 88.4 % and 88.0 % removal rates after 12 h dark reaction and 6 h light reaction treatment [13]. BC exhibits unique properties that can effectively suppress electron-hole recombination, facilitate electron tunneling, and immobilize contaminants, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics [14]. The advantages included high specific surface area, rich pore structure, and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, which helped BC adsorb more pollutants on the surface [15]. The adsorption would enhance the contact between target pollutants and active species (reactive radicals and holes), thereby improving the removal efficiency of pollutants [16]. The unmodified BC usually had less catalytic activity sites and low electron transfer efficiency, which hindered its application in photocatalysis [17]. By contrast, the modified BC overcame these problems and enhanced its photocatalytic efficiency.

The doping of heteroatoms and metals were usually used to improve the photocatalytic performance of BC, and Fe was a commonly used metal doping element to increase the electron transfer efficiency [18]. Doping BC with nitrogen elements could also enhance the photocatalytic performance by increasing nitrogen-related functional groups and tailoring the charge distribution of the sp2-hybridized carbon configuration [19]. Currently, the artful doping of Fe and N elements to form Fe-N-co-doped BC materials with exceptional catalytic effects has become a research focus. A magnetic N-doped iron sludge-based BC was used as raw material to activate persulfate for tetracycline degradation, and the removal rate was 86.56 % within 120 min [20]. Wolfberry, naturally rich in nitrogen and easily obtainable, serves as an ideal carbon and nitrogen-containing precursor, allowing the incorporation of its nitrogen elements into BC through hydrothermal carbonization. In addition, zero-dimensional nanomaterials carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could effectively increase the utilization of the full-spectrum of sunlight to accelerate the transfer of electrons due to their intrinsic nature of upcoversion [21,22]. CQDs-based heterojunctions have garnered significant attention for their integration with photocatalysts to enhance photoactivity [23,24]. P-n junction, type Ⅱ and Z-scheme heterojunction has proven to be an effective approach in promoting piezocatalytic activity [25,26]. The formation of a heterojunction between BC and CQDs significantly enhanced BC’s ability to absorb natural light, thereby boosting its photocatalytic activity [27]. Thus, we suspected that the construction of heterogeneous structures between BC and CQDs, and then doping them with Fe and N elements to composite photocatalytic materials might be a good choice to efficiently remove sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics in a more environmentally friendly and cost-effective way. Currently, research on CQDs and BC composite photocatalytic materials remains relatively limited, necessitating further in-depth exploration. The synergistic effects of the composite materials and their specific mechanisms in photocatalytic reactions have not yet been fully elucidated.

The purpose of this study was to one-step synthesize Fe-N-co-doped biochar/carbon quantum dots materials (Fe/N/BC/CQDs) for the removal of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics from the water under natural sunlight. Firstly, the Fe/N/BC/CQDs composite photocatalytic material was prepared and characterized. Secondly, photocatalytic degradation efficiency of sulfonamide (sulfathiazole (STZ), SMZ, sulfamonomethoxine (SMD), sulfadoxin (SDM), SM2) and fluoroquinolone (NOR) antibiotics by the Fe/N/BC/CQDs composite photocatalytic material was determined under two experimental conditions (with and without illumination). Furthermore, the degradation products of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics by the Fe/N/BC/CQDs materials were identified, and the mechanism of photocatalytic synergistic degradation was investigated by measuring free radical quenching experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra. This study would provide a theoretical basis for the application of biomass carbon-based catalytic materials to efficiently degrade antibiotics.

Wolfberry and K4[Fe(CN)6]·3H2O (≧99.5 %) were purchased from freshippo shopping mall (China) and Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. Sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The mobile phase and ultrapure water used to detect sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics were HPLC grades. The aquatic toxicology model organism Brachionus calyciflorus was obtained from our laboratory. All chemicals used in this experiment were of analytical grade.

The molecular structure, morphology, phase structure, chemical elements and states of elements, specific surface areas, and uv–vis absorption spectra of Fe/N/BC/CQDs were characterized using Fourier transform (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet iS50 FTIR, China), Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Jeol JSM-7610FPlus, Japan; Oxford ULTIM MAX 40, UK), X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Rigaku SmartLab 9 kW, Japan), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo K-alpha, US), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2460 2.02, US) and UV spectrophotometer (UV, Shimadzu UV 3600 Plus, Japan), respectively. The band-gap ratio of Fe/N/BC/CQDs was calculated using Tauc’s equation, as follows [28]:
(1)

A(hv - E g )

where a, h, v and Eg represent the absorption coefficient, Planck's Constant, optical frequency, and bandgap energy, respectively. A photoluminescence spectrometer (PL, Hitachi F-4600, Japan) was employed to investigate PL properties. The electrochemical workstation (CS312H, Corrtest Instrument Co. Ltd., Wuhan, China) was used to analyze photoelectric chemical properties of photocatalyst.

Mott-Schottky plot was employed using the standard three-electrode system, with saturated calomel electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode serving as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. Meanwhile, the homogeneous mixture prepared by milling 100 mg of catalyst, 2 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 20 µL of Nafion*D 521 dispersion was transferred to FTO glass as the working electrode.
Fluorescence spectrometer (F97pro Fluorescence Spectroscopy, China) for the N/CQDs and Fe/N/CQDs fluorescence test in which both excitation bandwidth and emission bandwidth were 10 nm.

Persistent free radicals in activators were measured through EPR (Magnettech MS 5000, German), in which 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone hydrochloride (TEMP) were used as spin-trapping agents.

The photocatalytic performance was examined by determining the photodegradation of 10 mg/L sulfonamide (STZ, SMZ, SMD, SDM, SM2) and fluoroquinolone (NOR) antibiotics in a working volume of 50 mL under natural light by 20 mg Fe/N/BC/CQDs. The intensity of natural sunlight irradiation was measured utilizing a calibrated photodiode linked to a voltmeter. In the present study, the average solar irradiation and temperature recorded during the experiments were 109 ± 21 mW cm−2 and 31.2°C, respectively. To reduce the effects of unavoidable fluctuations in solar light irradiance across different days, the experiments were executed in sets of simultaneous photocatalytic tests conducted in parallel.

Before illumination, the solution was stirred in the dark for 1 h to ensure the achievement of adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then the photocatalytic activity was performed by irradiating the mixture with visible light. After 0, 60, 120, 180, 240, 420, and 540 min, Samples of 100 µL solution (n = 6) were collected, diluted 10 times, and filtrated through 0.22 µm hydrophobic filter membranes for the following ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography tandem high-resolution mass spectrometry (HPLC-HRMS) analysis. The HPLC-HRMS condition and highly responsive and stable chromatograms were shown in Supplementary material (Method S1, Fig. S2 ). Meanwhile, sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics kept in the dark were collected at the same time point as the control. The photocatalytic efficiency (%) was determined by applying the following formula:
(2)

where C0 is the original concentration, and Ct is the concentrations of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics at time t.

The toxicity of antibiotics photocatalytic degradation intermediates was assessed by evaluating the 24-hour mortality rate of rotifers [29]. The detailed experimental procedure was shown in Supplementary material (Method S2).

The reusability of the Fe/N/BC/CQDs photocatalysts was assessed by washing them with deionized water followed by overnight drying at 60℃. The recycled Fe/N/BC/CQDs were reused in SMZ photocatalytic reactions under the same experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of Fe/N/BC/CQDs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfonamide (STZ, SMZ, SMD, SDM, SM2) and fluoroquinolone (NOR) antibiotics at a concentration of 10 mg/L were added into 50 mL tap water (pH = 6.5) and aquaculture tail water (pH = 6.5) and municipal wastewater (pH = 6.8), respectively. After the addition of 20 mg Fe/N/BC/CQDs, the mixture was placed in the dark for 1 h, followed by 8 h under natural light. After 0, 60, 120, 180, 240, 420, and 540 min, Samples of 100 µL solution (n = 8) were collected at each time point, diluted 10 times, and filtrated through 0.22 µm hydrophobic filter membranes for HPLC-HRMS analysis.

All experimental groups were subjected to at least 6 parallel experiments. Experimental data were represented as mean ± standard error. The Langmuir pseudo first-order model and modified Elovich model fitted by Origin software.

The FTIR spectrum of Fe/N/BC/CQDs and N/BC/CQDs illustrated bands around 3370 cm−1, the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H (Fig. 1
a), indicating the successful doping of N elements from wolfberry into Fe/N/BC/CQDs and N/BC/CQDs [30]. The absorption peaks at around 2360 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C=C and C=N/C=O, respectively. Compared to N/BC/CQDs, Fe/N/BC/CQDs showed a more prominent peak at 1590 cm−1, suggesting an increase in oxygen-containing groups after Fe doping. Oxygenated functional group represented a promising avenue to mitigate the inherently fast bulk recombination rates, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity [31]. The stretching vibration of Fe-O was observed at 621 cm−1 in the Fe/N/BC/CQDs, also demonstrating the doping of Fe on the Fe/N/BC/CQDs [32]. Additionally, absorption peaks at 2930 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 corresponded to the σC-H stretching vibration and δC-H in-plane bending vibration, respectively. It was noteworthy that the strength of σC-H and δC-H bonds increased due to changes in the molecular structure. Moreover, Fe doping promoted the formation of oxygen vacancy, which might lead to higher photocatalytic activity of Fe/N/BC/CQDs compare to N/BC/CQDs [33]. In addition, γC-H absorption peak at around 775 cm−1 was shown in the FTIR spectrum of Fe/N/BC/CQDs, indicating the formation of a benzene ring structure on Fe/N/BC/CQDs. The benzene ring structure would extend light adsorption and enhance separation/transfer of photoinduced holes and electrons [34]. These above results further confirmed that Fe doping greatly altered the properties of BC.

The XRD patterns of Fe/N/BC/CQDs illustrated a broad peak centered at 21.9° corresponding to the (002) plane with an amorphous nature (Fig. 1 b). This peak was related to the carbonization of the hydrothermal process, which generated the graphitic structure of BC [35]. The characteristic peaks at 2θ = 24.8°, 2θ = 30.9°, and 2θ = 35.5° corresponded to the (012), (104), and (110) planes, respectively, providing further evidence of Fe3+ doping [36]. Full scan XPS results showed that four elements (O, N, C, Fe) were present in Fe/N/BC/CQDs (Fig. 1 c), and 3 diffraction peaks at energy levels of 531.2 eV, 532.04 eV and 533.3 eV corresponded to Fe-O–H, C=O, O=C=O (Fig. 1 d). Among them, C=O groups were closely interrelated and formed persistent free radicals (PFRs), which could serve as focal points for redox reactions and potentially donate electrons to oxygen in the water. This process could generate •O2 – free radicals that effectively degrade contaminants [37]. The presence of newly formed Fe-O–H bonds suggested the active involvement of Fe in the construction of novel composite structures. The N1s peaks could be divided into three peaks at 401 eV, 399.92 eV, 398.67 eV, corresponding to C-N, N≡C, C-N=C respectively (Fig. 1 e). Specifically, the binding energies at 284.7 eV corresponded to the C=C (Fig. 1 f), which was consistent with the FTIR results.

The intensity of UV absorption peaks of Fe/N/BC/CQDs increased significantly after doping with Fe element. After loaded Fe, both the visible light response range and absorption intensity of the photocatalyst were significantly enhanced in the range of 200 − 800 nm, indicating good absorption of visible light (Fig. 1 g). The electron density distribution and energy gap were changed due to the Fe-O and various coordination bonds formed in Fe and CQDs [38].

The Eg was calculated as 1.51 eV, and photocatalysts with narrower energy gaps could utilize less energy and time for electronic excitation [39]. More importantly, the low defect density at heterojunction interfaces further promotes this advantage by minimizing recombination centers, resulting in prolonged e-h+ separation lifetime [40]. Thus, Fe/N/BC/CQDs could easily capture visible light. The valence band (VB) was obtained using Mott-Schottky curves and the Mott-Schottky plots exhibited both positive (k2 = 3.629) and negative (k1 = -4.088) tangent slopes (Fig. 1 h), indicating that Fe/N/BC/CQDs were confirmed as a p-n heterogeneous structure [41]. The p-n heterogeneous structure typically exhibited superior photocatalytic efficiency due to the concurrent doped of electron (N) and hole impurities (Fe). This led to the formation of a composite layer between electrons and holes, thereby enhancing their photocatalytic performance. Electrons could migrate from the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) band of CQDs to the conduction band (CB) of Fe-N-co-doped BC. Meanwhile, holes could move from the VB of Fe/N/BC to the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) of CQDs [42]. Consequently, this structure promoted efficient charge separation.

Photoluminescence emission spectroscopy (PL) was employed to investigate the rate of charge carrier capture, migration, and transfer in materials [43]. The Fe/N/BC/CQDs exhibited weaker PL signals compared to Fe/N/BC ( Fig. S3
a). The reduced PL intensity demonstrates enhanced charge transfer efficiency, confirming that Fe/N /CQDs doping effectively suppresses carrier recombination in the synthesized materials [44]. Photocurrent (I-t) measurements were conducted in a three-electrode system to further evaluate the catalytic performance. Typically, enhanced photocurrent generation correlates with suppressed charge carrier recombination kinetics in semiconductor photocatalysts. Fe/N/BC/CQDs demonstrated significantly higher photocurrent than Fe/N/BC, attributed to superior charge separation efficiency and absorption of visible-light ( Fig. S3
b) [45]. Fe/N/BC/CQDs had a smaller diameter compared to Fe/N/BC ( Fig. S3
c), which implies higher electronic conductivity (Nyquist plots of EIS). The incorporation of Fe/N/CQDs significantly enhanced the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers in the composite material [46].

The clear irregular pore structure indicated that the doping of hetero-elements led to defects in the graphitized structure of Fe/N/BC/CQDs (Fig. 1 i-j). Smooth crystal structures observed in Fe/N/BC/CQDs confirmed the successful doping of Fe species [47]. EDS analysis showed that the internal doping and surface metal loading were uniformly distributed throughout the material, with no significant agglomeration observed inside or outside. The spectrum of Fe/N/BC/CQDs showed that C was greatest at 73.16 %, followed by O (21.36 %), N (3.77 %), and Fe (1.71 %; Fig. 1 k-n).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics by Fe/N/BC/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics was elucidated using the Langmuir pseudo first-order model and modified Elovich model. The R2 values for the Elovich model were 0.9966, 0.9846, 0.9642, 0.9841, 0.9898, 0.9856 for NOR, STZ, SMZ, SMD, SDM, and SM2, respectively. In contrast, the R2 values for the Langmuir pseudo first-order model were 0.9844, 0.9611, 0.9401, 0.9799, 0.9768, 0.9733, respectively. By contrast, the modified Elovich model could describe the experimental data better than the Langmuir pseudo first-order model (Fig. 2 c-h and
Fig. S10 ). The better fit of the photocatalytic degradation data to the modified Elovich model indicated that the degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics was controlled by multiple processes rather than a single adsorption or photodegradation. This further demonstrated that the photocatalytic degradation involved a dynamic process of adsorption-photocatalytic degradation–adsorption. The degradation mechanism involved an intricate combination of initial rapid adsorption on high-energy active sites, followed by diffusion-controlled access to mesopores, and competitive radical-mediated oxidation pathways.

The molecular weight of NOR in the plot was m/z 320 peak decreased significantly, and two possible degradation pathways were proposed based on relevant literature and peaks in chromatograms (Fig. 3
a and
Fig. S11 ) [49]. The degradation of NOR could result from the disruption of the piperazine ring, possibly induced by the generation of •OH/h+ radicals in the solution [50]. The degradation pathway I of NOR began with the breakage of piperazine ring of parent compound, resulting in the formation of NO1 at the characteristic m/z 350 peak. –CO and −C2H4NH groups were removed from NO1, further developed NO2 (m/z 279). NO3 (m/z 251) was formed by the loss of –CO group. The piperazinyl substituent of NOR was entirely removed and transformed to NO4 (m/z 233), which underwent a series of decarboxylation and deamination reactions to form the aromatic compounds NO8 (m/z 154) and NO9 (m/z 114). The degradation pathway Ⅱ showed NOR first defluorinated and then disrupted the piperazine ring form intermediates NO5 (m/z 304), NO6 (m/z 336), NO7 (m/z 304). In the control group without the Fe/N/BC/CQDs material, NO1 − NO8 ion fragments were undetected, further confirming that NOR underwent photocatalytic degradation in the presence of the Fe/N/BC/CQDs material. As photocatalytic reaction time increased, NOR was eventually mineralized to CO2 and H2O (possible intermediates were listed in Table S2 ).

Pivotal pathway of STZ degradation contained the bond break and hydroxylation (Fig. 3 b, Fig. S12
and
Table S3
). ST1 (m/z 100) and ST2 (m/z 174) were generated by cleavage of the S-N bond by reactant attack, further ST1 formed ST3 (m/z 204) by hydroxylation [51].

The possible degradation pathway of SMZ (m/z 254) molecules was shown in Fig. 3 c, and possible intermediates and their mass spectra was shown in Table S4 and Fig. S13 . Since elemental N was the most active potentiometric site, it was easily attacked by 1O2 and h+ to form SMZ-1 (m/z 284) in pathway Ⅰ. SMZ-2 (m/z 99) was formed by further oxidation and degradation of SMZ-1. In pathway Ⅱ SMZ-3 (m/z 239) was formed due to the electrophilic reaction and hydroxyl group breakage [52].

The potential intermediates information was listed in Fig. S14 and Table S5 . SMD (m/z 279) was attracted by •OH radicals to form SMD-1 (m/z 295; Fig. 3 d), which were further degraded into SMD-2 (m/z 280; Fig. S14 and Table S5 ). •OH radicals induced the breakage of the C-N bond with the simultaneous generation of two intermediates, SMD-3 (m/z 109) and SMD-4 (m/z 205). Subsequently, the benzene ring of SMD-4 was broken and converted to SMD-5 (m/z 165). The end products were mineralized into harmless products such as CO2 and H2O [53].

In degradation pathway I of SDM (Fig. 3 e), the amino bond was broken, and a methyl group was added to the C atom attached to the N atom in the pyrimidine ring. This was followed by hydroxylation and oxidation, culminating in the formation of SD1 (m/z 324). Breakage of SDM internal bonds led to the formation of SD2 (m/z 246). Degradation pathway Ⅲ showed the hydroxylation of the aniline ring to form SD3 (m/z 327) [54]. The potential intermediates information was listed in Fig. S15 and Table S6 .

These pathways demonstrate the stepwise degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics through various processes including the disruption of the piperazine ring, decarboxylation, deamination, bond break, hydroxylation and further fragmentation. The degradation is primarily driven by the attack of reactive species, including •O2 –, h+ , •OH and 1O2.

The degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics (10 mg/L) was inhibited by the addition of PBQ, EDTA-2Na, IPA and FFA, confirming that this photocatalytic degradation reaction was controlled by free radicals (Fig. 4
a-f). The degradation rates of NOR, STZ, SMZ, SMD, SDM, and SM2 with the addition of PBQ was 30.85 %, 35.43 %, 36.88 %, 38.94 %, 32.00 %, 32.09 %, respectively. The degradation rates of NOR, STZ, SMZ, SMD, SDM, and SM2 with the addition of EDTA-2Na was 44.18 %, 44.02 %, 47.82 %, 48.91 %, 47.81 %, 48.75 %, respectively. The degradation rates of NOR, STZ, SMZ, SMD, SDM, and SM2 with the addition of IPA was 78.42 %, 69.87 %, 78.04 %, 68.04 %, 68.06 %, 61.09 %, respectively. The degradation rates of NOR, STZ, SMZ, SMD, SDM, and SM2 with the addition of FFA was 57.43 %, 51.98 %, 63.84 %, 55.69 %, 53.98 %, 53.51 %, respectively. In addition, the addition of a radical scavenger resulted in varying degrees of decrease in the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. It was speculated that •O2 –, h+ , •OH and 1O2 played important roles in the degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics, and the free radicals responsible for the degradation followed the order of •O2 –> h+ > 1O2> •OH. This could be attributed to the fact that Fe/N/BC/CQDs produced the highest concentration of •O2 – radicals in solution through photocatalytic.

Six lines of equal intensity of DMPO-O2 •– were observed in the DMPO-methanol solution ( Fig. 4 g), and the high peak intensities of DMPO-O2 •– indicated that more free •O2 – radicals were produced, which was consisted with the results of the above-mentioned radical trapping experiments [56]. The weaker signal 1:2:2:1 corresponded to the DMPO-•OH adduct ( Fig. 4 g) was also observed [57]. The EPR results showed three typical TEMPO-1O2 peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 (Fig. 4 h), which was consistent with those of radical trapping. This suggested the presence of three distinct types of free radicals within the mixed system, and the –O2 •– free radical was the more abundant species.

The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics by Fe/N/BC/CQDs was shown in Fig. 5 . After the absorption of light, the excited electrons in Fe/N/BC transition from the VB to the CB. When the energy of a photon matched or exceeded the bandgap energy of Fe/N/BC, the h+ would be left in the VB. The photo-generated e-/h+ were separated, reacted with O2 and H2O at the solid–liquid interface, and produced large amounts of •O2 –, •OH [58]. Fe and N doping facilitated the formation of free radicals. The fabricated band edge alignment between Fe/N/BC and Fe/N/CQDs allowed the establishment of a heterojunction configuration. The electrons could smoothly migrate from the LUMO band of Fe/N/CQDs to the CB of Fe/N/BC, while holes could effectively transfer from the VB of Fe/N/BC to the HOMO of Fe/N/CQDs. The incorporation of Fe/N/CQDs in this composite not only enhanced the efficiency of absorption and utilization of visible light, but also facilitated the formation of electron-hole pairs and indirectly boosted the production of free radicals in solution. Meanwhile, sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics were adsorbed on the surface of Fe/N/BC by electrostatic and van der Waals forces, which improved the photocatalytic efficiency through the synergistic adsorption-catalytic effect [59].
BC + hv → BC (e- ) + BC (h+ )(3)
e- + O2 →•O2 –(4)
h+ + H2O →•OH + H+(5)
h+ + OH– →•OH(6)

The practical application of Fe/N/BC/CQDs was verified through experiments in tap water and aquaculture tail water and municipal wastewater. The key physicochemical properties of the three types were summarized in Table S8 . The degradation rates of Fe/N/BC/CQDs for NOR, STZ, SMZ, SMD, SDM, SM2 ranged from 78.81 % to 95.30 % in the tap water and 76.21 % to 92.51 % in aquaculture tail water and 74.78 % to 88.24 % in municipal wastewater ( Fig. S18 ). The removal rates of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics by Fe/N/BC/CQDs in deionized water, tap water and aquaculture tail water were not significantly different. The quality of the tap water was relatively clear, with minimal suspended solids, thus had a negligible impact on the removal rate. Aquaculture tailwater had higher turbidity and contained more complex components, however it still had little effect on the photocatalytic degradation process of antibiotics by Fe/N/BC/CQDs. Fe/N/BC/CQDs composites showed slightly diminished removal rates for sulfonamides and fluoroquinolones in municipal wastewater matrices, likely due to competitive adsorption with coexisting organics. The system maintained remarkable strong photocatalytic performance even under complex water matrices. The results demonstrated a strong anti-interference ability of Fe/N/BC/CQDs in real water environments, making them suitable for the degradation of antibiotics in wastewater.

In summary, a Fe/N/BC/CQDs composites were successfully synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal method. This method achieved efficient antibiotic degradation under natural sunlight through three synergistic mechanisms. Firstly, the incorporation of Fe/N/CQDs improved the efficiency of visible light absorption and utilization of this material. Secondly, the pore structure of the biochar led to a dynamic equilibrium process of adsorption-photocatalytic degradation. The degradation efficiencies of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics ranged from 74.21 % to 89.23 %, achieved under the conditions of a 1 h dark reaction followed by 3 h of natural light. Thirdly, the in situ formation of p-n heterojunction capable of inducing the generation of •O2 –, h+ , 1O2, •OH free radicals for photocatalytic degradation. The Fe/N/BC/CQDs composite demonstrates remarkable performance improvements over traditional photocatalysts. The Fe/N/BC/CQDs composite demonstrates high degradation efficiency for sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics even in complex real water systems, indicating its strong anti-interference capability in practical aquatic environments. This makes it highly suitable for antibiotic degradation in wastewater. This method provides a methodological reference for green and efficient removal of sulfonamide and fluoroquinolone antibiotics from water.
